projects.name: ScrySheets

textSections.title: Overview
textSections.text: ScrySheets is an Excel-based deck building and card management tool for Magic: The Gathering. Built using VBA, Power Query and the Scryfall API, it automatically imports card data into a searchable database and streamlines the process of creating, organizing and pricing decks. The project was developed to explore how spreadsheet software can be extended into a functional application through automation and custom user interfaces.

textSections.title: Process
textSections.text: The project began with a Power Query pipeline that retrieves and refreshes card data directly from the Scryfall API. Magic is an ever-expanding game with new cards being added constantly and fluctuating prices so I wanted and option to have the database be dynamic. The database sends a requests for the name of the most recent ScryFall database, then it sends a new API call using the most recent name to fetch the most recent bulk data. VBA macros were then developed to handle card searching, deck construction, printing and database management. The card searcher takes input from the user and finds card objects from the database and appends them to the deck-building section based on card type. I built in user-validation for partial matching of card names as well as a suggestion bar for similar entries. Deck statistics such as price breakdown per card type are made into graphs and displayed below to better represent the data.

projects.shortDescription: Card searcher and database built in Excel

textSections.title: Overview
textSections.text:
As part of a multidisciplinary engineering design project, our team developed a scaled-down walking wheelchair prototype intended to explore alternative accessibility solutions for navigating terrain that conventional wheelchairs struggle to traverse. Inspired by quadrupedal locomotion, the robot utilized a TrotBot linkage mechanism to convert rotational motor motion into a walking gait capable of overcoming small obstacles and uneven surfaces.

	The project was evaluated through a series of engineering benchmarks including walking speed, obstacle traversal, incline climbing, payload capacity, and maneuverability. My role focused on electronics integration, embedded programming, mechanical design, and project coordination. Through multiple design iterations, the final prototype achieved substantial improvements in speed, payload capacity, traction, and overall reliability compared to the initial midterm prototype.

textSections.title: Design & Development
textSections.text:
Development began with a proof-of-concept prototype constructed from wooden linkages, DC gear motors, and a wired power supply. While the initial design successfully demonstrated the walking mechanism, testing revealed several engineering challenges including excessive weight, insufficient motor torque, high friction within the linkage system, and manufacturing inaccuracies caused by manually fabricated components. These findings guided the redesign and established the performance targets for the final prototype.

	I was responsible for sourcing the motors, batteries, Arduino control hardware, motor drivers, and supporting electronics. The robot was controlled using an Arduino Uno paired with an Adafruit Motor Shield, allowing independent control of four DC motors that drove the quadrupedal TrotBot linkage mechanism. I developed and refined the Arduino control software responsible for coordinating motor sequencing and gait timing. A major focus of the redesign was the power system, where we transitioned from a cumbersome wired setup to lightweight 18650 lithium-ion cells connected in series. I also tuned the DC-DC voltage converter to safely maximize the voltage supplied to the motors, increasing available torque and significantly improving overall performance.

	Mechanically, the robot was redesigned around a fully 3D-printed PLA chassis and linkage system. Precision-manufactured components eliminated alignment issues present in the wooden prototype, reducing friction and improving efficiency throughout the mechanism. Additional improvements included custom motor mounting arms, reinforced structural components, and redesigned feet with increased contact area for improved traction and stability. Alongside my technical contributions, I coordinated project milestones, delegated tasks across team members, and helped guide the project from concept to final testing.

textSections.title: Testing & Results
textSections.text:
Both the midterm and final prototypes were evaluated using identical benchmark tests, allowing performance improvements to be measured quantitatively. Following the redesign, the robot completed the 1 m walking test in 12.51 seconds compared to 21.58 seconds for the original prototype, representing an improvement of approximately 42%. Payload drag capacity increased from 1.40 kg to 2.18 kg, while payload carrying capacity improved from 0.29 kg to 0.88 kg.

	The redesigned power system, lighter structure, reduced mechanical friction, and improved traction collectively resulted in a more capable walking platform that performed significantly better on uneven terrain and obstacle courses. The project demonstrated how iterative engineering design, data-driven testing, and multidisciplinary collaboration can transform a proof-of-concept prototype into a substantially more capable system.

mediaSections.title: Midterm Prototype and Roller Terrain Testing

projects.name: 1991 Mazda Miata

textSections.title: Overview
textSections.text: With the goal of learning the basics of 3D modeling, projection designing and assembly mates, I undertook the task of modeling my favorite car, the 1991 MX-5 in SolidWorks. Using reference pictures, I modeled each static panel of the chassis. I then created separate components for moving parts such as the swing doors, spinning wheels, the hydraulic trunk and the iconic winking headlights. While not an engineering-precise drawing, the model is complete enough to be exported and rendered, opening the door to creative applications such as advertisement visuals, producing 3D printed miniatures and more.

textSections.title: Process
textSections.text:
I began by projecting to-scale reference images of the front, side and top views onto their corresponding axes in SolidWorks. Using the 3D Sketch feature, I was then able to draw freely in space rather than being confined to a single plane. By locking the camera to one reference view at a time, I sketched each body panel along that projection, then switched to another view to adjust and vary the curvature. This created a 3D boundary that curved and bent in space according to all three references simultaneously.

	To fill these boundaries into actual surfaces, I used Surface Loft and Fill Surface, then applied Thicken to give each panel its physical form. This process was repeated for each non-moving body panel on one half of the car, which was then mirrored to complete the symmetry. Separate components such as the wheels, steering wheel, trunk, seats and headlights, were modeled independently using extrusions built up from basic shapes and brought together in the final assembly.

mediaSections.title: To scale reference pictures projected on each axis

projects.shortDescription: 3D assembly of a 1991 Mazda Miata modeled in SolidWorks

sections.title: Who I Am
sections.text:
I'm a Mechanical Engineering student at Concordia University, expected to graduate in 2029. My background spans engineering, IT and web development, giving me a unique mix of technical and problem-solving experience. From building computers and developing websites to creating CAD models and prototypes, I've always enjoyed turning ideas into practical solutions.

	Before pursuing engineering, I worked in IT, earned my CompTIA A+ certification and supported networking, POS and security systems for businesses across Vancouver. Through hands-on projects and industry experience, I discovered a passion for design and engineering that ultimately brought me to Montreal. Today, I'm focused on developing my skills, gaining industry experience and continuing to learn wherever I can.

sections.title: Skills & Experience
sections.text:
A growing foundation in mechanical engineering supported by experience in CAD modeling, prototyping and technical problem solving. My background in IT and web development allows me to work comfortably across both hardware and software environments.

	I bring strong communication skills, experience working with clients and teams, and fluency in English, French and Vietnamese.

Engineering: SolidWorks
Programming: C, C++, JavaScript, HTML, CSS
Development: Frontend & Backend, Git, MySQL
Productivity: Microsoft 365, Word, Excel, PowerPoint
Collaboration: Microsoft Teams

sections.title: Looking Ahead
sections.text: I'm seeking opportunities to gain hands-on engineering experience, learn from industry professionals and contribute to meaningful projects. Outside of school, you'll usually find me training for triathlons, playing badminton or enjoying a board game with friends. If you'd like to connect, feel free to reach out.
